title: Never Miss a Sunset With SunsetAlertPro™
author: Sponsored
snippet: Our patented algorithm predicts the peak color moment of every sunset within a 40km radius and pushes a 45-second warning to your phone. Includes a Grief Mode for clouds that never came.
full_text:
Our patented algorithm predicts the peak color moment of every sunset within a 40km radius and pushes a 45-second warning to your phone. Includes a Grief Mode for clouds that never came.

Free tier: one alert per week. Pro tier ($4.99/mo): unlimited alerts, heatmap of historical banger sunsets, social leaderboard. Enterprise tier: your company logo projected onto the cloud layer. Ask about our wildfire seasonal discount.

title: The one after my mother's funeral
author: Carla Rossi
author_bio: Ligurian coast, writing this from her kitchen table
snippet: We buried her at noon. By evening I was alone on the terrace with a glass of wine she had bought. The sky went orange and then pink and then a color I had no word for, and I understood that she had seen this a thousand times without me.
full_text:
We buried her at noon. By evening I was alone on the terrace with a glass of wine she had bought. The sky went orange and then pink and then a color I had no word for, and I understood that she had seen this a thousand times without me.

I am not religious. I did not feel her presence. I felt her absence, and I felt the sunset. Those are two different things and they can be in a room together. I sat until the color was gone. Then I washed the glass and went to bed. That is my favorite sunset. It is also the one I wish I did not have.

title: Somewhere over Greenland at 38,000 feet
author: Captain James Oladele
author_bio: 747 pilot, 28 years on the polar routes
snippet: From the flight deck at cruise you see the curvature of the earth eat the sun sideways. Twenty-six minutes from first reddening to full dark. The ice below turns the color of a peach and then the color of a bruise.
full_text:
From the flight deck at cruise you see the curvature of the earth eat the sun sideways. Twenty-six minutes from first reddening to full dark. The ice below turns the color of a peach and then the color of a bruise.

People ask me what it is like to fly for a living and I tell them about meal trays and jet lag and the paperwork. I do not tell them about the Greenland sunsets because they belong to the job in a way that does not translate. My first officers always go quiet. Nobody takes a picture. The picture would lie.

title: The one I almost missed because I was checking Instagram
author: Dev P.
snippet: I had the phone out trying to upload a photo of the PREVIOUS sunset. A monk tapped my shoulder and pointed west. I put the phone away. I have not had a favorite sunset since the one I was not posting.
full_text:
I had the phone out trying to upload a photo of the PREVIOUS sunset. A monk tapped my shoulder and pointed west. I put the phone away. I have not had a favorite sunset since the one I was not posting.

The irony is the photo from the day before was fine. Ten thousand likes. Nobody remembers it including me. The one I actually watched lives in my head in full resolution and I cannot show you. I think this is the correct trade but I am not sure it feels good.

title: The wildfire ones are the best and I hate myself for saying this
author: Dr. Lindsay Pham
snippet: Particulate matter in the 2–10 micron range scatters red and orange light spectacularly. When the Camp Fire smoke reached the Bay, the sunsets were objectively the most beautiful I have witnessed in my professional lifetime. I took pictures. I felt sick.
full_text:
Particulate matter in the 2–10 micron range scatters red and orange light spectacularly. When the Camp Fire smoke reached the Bay, the sunsets were objectively the most beautiful I have witnessed in my professional lifetime. I took pictures. I felt sick.

My favorite sunset is a moral compromise. My brain registered the light as sublime while my training told me exactly what I was looking at. I do not think beauty is supposed to feel like grief but that is where we are living now. Ask me again in ten years and the answer may be a sunset I have not yet seen through cleaner air.

title: My grandfather's porch in Mississippi, approximately every night from 1962 to 1998
author: Theodore Wright
snippet: He sat in the same chair. I sat on the step. The sun went behind the pecan tree and lit the whole yard gold. Sometimes he said 'there it is' and sometimes he said nothing. The sunset was the same sunset every time and every time it was different and that is the answer.
full_text:
He sat in the same chair. I sat on the step. The sun went behind the pecan tree and lit the whole yard gold. Sometimes he said 'there it is' and sometimes he said nothing. The sunset was the same sunset every time and every time it was different and that is the answer.

I have seen sunsets in Santorini, Uluru, Big Sur. I understand why people travel for them. But a favorite sunset is not the rarest one. It is the one you sat through most patiently. My grandfather did not know he was teaching me that. He was just tired after work.

title: The one at the end of our first date
author: Iris Bekele
snippet: We were on the roof of a parking garage because it was the only place open. The sky was mostly polluted haze and a strip of orange near the horizon. He said 'this is not a very good sunset' and I laughed and that is when I knew.
full_text:
We were on the roof of a parking garage because it was the only place open. The sky was mostly polluted haze and a strip of orange near the horizon. He said 'this is not a very good sunset' and I laughed and that is when I knew.

People want their favorite sunset to be a postcard. Mine is a mediocre sunset that happened to land during a moment when I started to love someone. The sunset is doing almost none of the work in that memory and I cannot separate it from the rest of it and I do not want to. We were married last year. The wedding sunset was nicer. It is not the favorite.
